hub_subtitle: Business English para as reuniões com o time Global da Sanofi, congressos médicos e a apresentação de agosto

lesson.menu_title: Your Day and Your Work Routine -- A Week at Sanofi Brazil
lesson.menu_desc: Describing your typical week to the Global team: schedule, field visits, reports -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 2 -- Your Day and Your Work Routine
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Emmanuele Orrico | Aula 2 -- Your Day and Your Work Routine

listenings.text: Hello Emmanuele. Marc Dubois again, from Paris. I have one small request before August. The Global team wants to understand your week. Not the numbers. The week. How often do you meet your field team? How often do you travel? When do you prepare the reports? Please send me five or six simple sentences. Every week I do this. Every month I do that. Short sentences are perfect. Nobody here expects perfect English. See you soon.

Good morning, everyone, and welcome to the Monday call. My name is Claire Bennett, and this is the agenda for the week. On Monday, we always review the demand plan with each country. On Tuesday, the medical reps send their field reports. On Wednesday, we usually prepare the slides for the global review. On Thursday, every country attends the immunology call at three o'clock, Paris time. And on Friday, we never schedule meetings. Friday is for the reports. If you travel this week, please tell your manager. Thank you, and have a good week.

extra_audio.text: On Monday, I always review the demand plan with my team. On Tuesday, I usually read the field reports. On Wednesday, I often do a field visit with a medical rep. On Thursday, I always attend the global call. On Friday, I prepare the presentation for the next week.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: the person who talks to doctors about our medicines
to look at something again and check it
the plan of your week: what happens and when
the main office of the company
a day outside the office, with the team, visiting doctors
a document with numbers and information about the business
to go to a meeting or a call
the plan that says how much medicine the market needs

reading.rtitle: A Week in the Life of a National Demand Manager
paras: Emmanuele always starts her week with numbers. On Monday morning, she reviews the demand plan with her team. She checks the schedule for the week and she reads the first reports. "Monday is never quiet," she says. "It is part of the job."

On Tuesday and Wednesday, she often goes out of the office. She does a field visit with a medical rep, and together they visit doctors in hospitals and clinics. She usually comes back with new information about the patients. She does not stay at her desk every day. "I need to see the market with my own eyes," she says.

On Thursday, she always attends the global call with headquarters in Paris. She listens, she answers questions, and she presents the numbers for her disease areas. On Friday, she prepares the reports for the next week. She sometimes travels to other cities, but she never travels on Fridays. Friday is for the reports, the plan, and the quiet work.

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Emmanuele works only with numbers and never leaves her office.
Emmanuele has a clear weekly routine: numbers on Monday, the field in the middle of the week, the global call on Thursday, and reports on Friday.
Emmanuele travels every day of the week and has no routine at all.

items: Emmanuele reviews the demand plan on Monday.
The text says she reviews the demand plan with her team on Monday morning.
She always stays at her desk and never visits doctors.
She often goes out of the office and does field visits with a medical rep.
She attends the global call with headquarters on Thursday.
The third paragraph says she always attends the global call on Thursday.
She never travels on Fridays -- Friday is for the reports.

the reports for the next week, and she never

gapfill.text: Notice the small words before the verb: always, usually, often, sometimes, never. They tell your Global colleagues HOW OFTEN, and they go before the main verb: she always reviews, she never travels.

lf.title: Where does the small word go?
items: review the demand plan on Monday.
sends the field reports on Tuesday.

lf.text: Notice: always, usually, often, sometimes and never come BEFORE the main verb (I always review, we never schedule). Always and never are the strong ones -- 100% and 0%. Usually, often and sometimes are in the middle.

bank.label: Useful language for describing your week
items: I usually start my day with...
I attend the global call every Thursday.
I never schedule meetings on Friday.
It is part of the job.

tips: Every week, I review the demand plan.
On Mondays, I always meet my team.
I sometimes travel to other cities.

items.situation: "When do you meet your field team?"
tips: I usually do a field visit on Wednesday.
My medical reps send their reports on Tuesday.

items.situation: "Do you attend the global call every week?"
tips: Yes, I attend the global call every Thursday.

items.situation: "And when do you prepare the reports?"
tips: I prepare the reports on Friday.
I never prepare them on Monday -- Monday is for the plan.

items.situation: Your week was hard and the Global team asks how it went. Answer, and use the expression from today.
tips: It was a busy week. It is part of the job.
Some weeks are hard. It is part of the job.
